deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 62-year-old man with sarcoidosis and heart disease.
Let me go through the case step by step.
He presented with new-onset diplopia and weakness.
Before that, he had nausea, dizziness, tinnitus, and unsteady gait, along with subjective fevers.
Five days before admission, he had a respiratory infection with inner-ear symptoms, which improved transiently.
Then, three days before, he noticed muscle issues, more severe in his arms, shaking hands, and trouble rising.
He was admitted to another hospital where his arm weakness fluctuated and he had dysarthria, which lessened with testing.
His labs: normal CBC, differential, ESR, electrolytes, glucose, and C-reactive protein.
Renal and liver functions were normal.
Chest X-ray showed small bilateral pleural effusions.
CT of the head and neck were normal.
CSF had 4 RBCs, 188 WBCs, with 88% lymphocytes.
EMG was normal.
He was treated with antibiotics and prednisone.
During his stay, he developed horizontal diplopia that got better when one eye was covered.
Then he had sweats and chills.
He was transferred to this hospital.
On admission, he had diplopia, more pronounced arm tremors, increased urinary frequency, night sweats, and chills.
No other symptoms like numbness or pain.
He has a history of cardiac sarcoidosis diagnosed 16 months prior with PET/CT showing increased FDG uptake in lymph nodes.
Pathology showed confluent granulomas with hyalinization, no organisms on staining.
He's been on prednisone and methotrexate recently.
He has other issues: hyperlipidemia, CAD with stents, CHF, complete heart block, ICD, asthma, allergies, and recent dental surgery with antibiotics.
He's on several medications, including statins, beta-blockers, aspirin, vitamins, inhalers, enoxaparin, and sometimes ipratropium and nitroglycerin.
On exam, he's alert and oriented, slightly febrile, with subtle memory issues.
Pupils reactive, no nystagmus.
Horizontal diplopia, right eyelid ptosis, coarse nystagmus.
Motor exam shows myoclonus in arms, normal strength, deep tendon reflexes 1+, normal sensation, gait not tested.
Blood tests: normal troponin, B12, folate, cortisol.
CSF showed increased lymphocytes, some neutrophils, proteinaceous debris.
Sputum Gram's stain shows few leukocytes, some squamous cells, and flora.
Blood and urine cultures were negative.
He developed somnolence, dysarthria, diplopia, right eyelid ptosis, and nystagmus.
CSF changes, and then on the fourth day, he had hyponatremia and progressive weakness.
CT scans showed brain edema and white matter changes.
EEG showed delta activity and triphasic waves.
He was intubated and moved to ICU.
Tests for autoimmune markers were negative.
He was treated with more steroids, amiodarone, and amphotericin.
Looking at the differential diagnosis, the main possibilities are neurocorticosteroid response, brain infection, or autoimmune encephalopathy.
Given his history of sarcoidosis, which is a systemic granulomatous disease, it's possible that this is a neurosarcoidosis presentation.
Sarcoidosis can affect the brain, causing neurological symptoms like diplopia, tremors, and myoclonus.
The CSF showing lymphocytosis and proteinaceous debris could support this.
The brain imaging changes, especially the white matter edema and leptomeningeal enhancement, are also consistent with neurosarcoidosis.
Other possibilities include viral encephalitis, but the lack of CSF pleocytosis (though there are some neutrophils now) and negative viral tests makes it less likely.
Autoimmune encephalitis could be considered, but the lack of autoantibodies and normal CSF initially makes it less likely.
Hypothyroidism is less likely as thyroid function was normal.
Given his history of sarcoidosis and the presence of diplopia, tremors, myoclonus, and CSF changes, the most likely diagnosis is neurosarcoidosis.

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 62-year-old man with sarcoidosis and heart disease who presented with diplopia, weakness, and other symptoms.
Let me go through the case step by step.
First, the patient has a history of sarcoidosis, which is a systemic granulomatous disease.
He also has cardiac issues, including cardiac sarcoidosis diagnosed 16 months prior.

He was on prednisone and methotrexate recently, which makes me think about possible side effects or complications from these medications, but his symptoms started after stopping those, so maybe not directly related.
His initial symptoms included nausea, dizziness, tinnitus, and unsteady gait, which were associated with fevers.
He was diagnosed with a respiratory infection with inner-ear symptoms, but those improved transiently.
Then, he developed muscle weakness, more pronounced in the arms, difficulty moving, and tremors.
The weakness fluctuated, which is a bit unusual for many neurologic conditions.
On exam, he had horizontal diplopia, ptosis, and tremors.
Cranial nerves seemed okay except for the diplopia and ptosis.
Motor exam showed myoclonus and some weakness.
His reflexes were normal, and sensation was intact.
The tremors were worse with eyes open, which makes me think of cerebellar or extrapyramidal issues, but the fluctuating weakness is more concerning.
The CSF analysis showed elevated white cells (188, mostly lymphocytes) and a few red cells.
This suggests a possible central nervous system (CNS) infection or inflammation.
The fact that the CSF was abnormal makes me think of conditions like meningitis, encephalitis, or maybe a neuroinvasive disease.
He had a chest X-ray showing pleural effusions, but no consolidation or pneumothorax.
CT of the head and neck were normal initially, but later CT showed sulcal effacement and hypodensities in white matter, which could indicate edema or swelling.
The leptomeningeal enhancement and increased ventricular size on later imaging suggest some kind of meningeal or parenchymal process.
His symptoms progressed to include fevers, confusion, and eventually required intubation.
The CSF analysis repeated showed more cells, including neutrophils now, which could indicate a shift towards bacterial infection.
However, cultures were negative, which makes it less likely to be a bacterial meningitis.
The fact that he was on broad-spectrum antibiotics and antifungals didn't seem to help much.
The patient's history of sarcoidosis is significant because sarcoidosis can affect multiple organs, including the CNS.
Neurosarcoidosis is a possibility here.
It can present with a variety of neurologic symptoms, including cranial nerve issues, encephalopathy, and myoclonus.
The CSF findings with elevated lymphocytes could be consistent with neurosarcoidosis, which often shows a lymphocytic pleocytosis.
Another possibility is a viral encephalitis, but the lack of a clear viral trigger and the chronic nature of his symptoms make this less likely.
The patient's recent dental surgery could have exposed him to some infection, but the cultures didn't show any growth, which argues against a bacterial or fungal infection.
The fluctuating weakness and the presence of myoclonus also make me think of other conditions like multiple sclerosis (MS), but the patient doesn't have the typical history or MRI findings for MS.
The CSF in MS usually shows oligoclonal bands, which weren't mentioned here.
Considering his sarcoidosis and the neurologic symptoms, neurosarcoidosis seems like the most likely diagnosis.
It can cause a range of neurologic issues, including those described, and the CSF findings are consistent with an inflammatory process in the CNS.
The treatment with steroids and other immunosuppressive agents also aligns with managing neurosarcoidosis.
I should also consider other possibilities like Guillain-Barré syndrome, but that typically presents with ascending weakness and sensory symptoms, which aren't as prominent here.
The presence of diplopia and cranial nerve issues makes me think more towards a central nervous system process rather than a peripheral one.

In summary, the combination of his history of sarcoidosis, the neurologic symptoms, the CSF findings, and the imaging changes pointing towards CNS involvement all point towards neurosarcoidosis as the diagnosis.
